nativeRange.habitat: Found on rubble and weedy bottoms of seaward reefs, coastal reef slopes, and estuarine reef margins, typically at depths of about 6 to 35 metres.
nativeRange.biotope: In the wild, social groups consisting of multiple females, juveniles, and one or more males range over extensive sections of mixed-substrate tropical reef.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and crevices for retreat

diet.notes: Generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis, frozen brine shrimp and other finely chopped meaty seafoods alongside a quality marine flake/pellet. Small, frequent feedings (2-3 times daily) tend to maintain coloration and condition.

avoidWith: triggers and large angelfish that may bully them
predatory fish that could eat a small wrasse
multiple males of the same species in a single tank

summary: The Yellowfin Flasher Wrasse is a small, peaceful reef fish from the eastern Indian Ocean, recognized by its vivid yellow ventral fins and the intense courtship displays males perform at dusk. Generally considered reef-safe and easy to keep, it suits peaceful community reef tanks with a tight-fitting lid.
careNotes: The Yellowfin Flasher Wrasse (Paracheilinus flavianalis) is a small species reaching around 7 to 9 cm in length, found across the eastern Indian Ocean from Indonesia to northwestern Australia, where social groups range over rubble, weedy bottoms, and coastal reef slopes between roughly 6 and 35 metres depth. Males display characteristic bright yellow anal and pelvic fins that intensify dramatically during twilight 'flashing' courtship displays. Like other flasher wrasses in the genus Paracheilinus, this species is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite — meaning individuals begin life as females and the dominant fish transitions to male — and forms harems in the wild. Aquarists generally keep one male alongside a small group of two to five females, introducing the females first or simultaneously to encourage settled behaviour and full male coloration. Keeping more than one male in the same aquarium is widely considered inadvisable, as males tend to fight. A tight-fitting lid or mesh top is essential, as flasher wrasses are well-known jumpers and have been reported lost from open-topped tanks. Generally regarded as peaceful, reef-safe, and relatively easy to keep, this species poses no documented threat to corals or sessile invertebrates and tends to accept standard prepared marine foods readily.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pairing during breeding; males perform elaborate twilight courtship displays with intensified yellow fin coloration. Captive spawning is occasionally reported but rearing the small pelagic larvae is generally considered difficult and not routinely accomplished in home aquaria.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary species product page. Provided care level (Easy), reef compatibility (Yes), temperament (Peaceful), max size (3.5 in / ~8.9 cm), minimum tank (55 US gal / ~208 L), water parameters (72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12), diet (carnivore: vitamin-enriched mysis/brine/flake), jumping risk (tight canopy recommended), and haremic recommendation (one male per group of females, introduced first).

additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific authority (Kuiter & Allen, 1999), family Labridae, order Eupercaria, max length 8.5 cm TL, depth range 6-35 m, native distribution (Indonesia, northwestern Australia in the eastern Indian Ocean), preferred temperature 26.4-28.6°C (mean 27.8°C), oviparous with distinct pairing, IUCN Least Concern (2009). Temperature spread vs LiveAquaria (22.2-25.6°C) reconciled by averaging to 23-27°C.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive spawning is occasional; rearing the small pelagic larvae is generally considered difficult and not routinely accomplished in home aquaria.
